title: Governance & Risk
summary: Defines where agents may act, how humans remain accountable, what controls are enforced, and how autonomy expands safely over time.
whyItMatters: Without governance, agentic delivery creates unmanaged operational, security, compliance, and business risk. Governance provides the boundaries, approvals, evidence, and escalation design that let autonomy scale inside enterprise controls.
executiveQuestions: What use cases are permitted for agentic execution?
What level of autonomy is acceptable by risk tier?
What requires human approval, escalation, or exception handling?
What tools, environments, and data may each agent access?
What evidence must be preserved for audit, compliance, and incident review?
keyDecisions: Risk-tier each agentic use case based on impact, reversibility, and data sensitivity
Define allowed actions, prohibited actions, and approval thresholds
Assign accountable business, technical, risk, and operational owners
Set identity, access, and blast-radius boundaries for agents and tools
Decide how autonomy can expand as control evidence matures
majorActivities: Create AI and agent acceptable-use policies
Map approval points across design, build, release, operate, and evolve
Implement policy as code, least privilege, and tool guardrails
Establish audit trails, decision logs, and compliance evidence packs
Run governance reviews after incidents, escalations, or control failures

customSections.title: Risk & use-case governance
customSections.description: Agentic AI systems introduce new operational and data risks due to their autonomy and ability to take actions. Organizations must evaluate use-case suitability, define risk tiers, and bound agents' capabilities through explicit limits, permissions, and identity controls.

items.title: Suitability
items.description: Determines whether a given task or business function is appropriate for agentic automation based on impact, autonomy level, and operational risk.
bullets: Evaluate whether the task requires human judgment, ethics, or high-stakes decision making.
Prefer bounded workflows with clear inputs, outputs, and success criteria.
Avoid autonomous execution in domains with legal or safety consequences.
Assess data sensitivity and external system impact before enabling agents.

items.title: Risk tiering
items.description: Classifies agentic systems by potential impact so that governance controls scale with the risk level of the use case.
bullets: Categorize agents by business impact, system access, and decision authority.
Apply stricter approval and monitoring for high-risk agents.
Define different control requirements for informational vs operational agents.
Continuously reassess risk as agents gain capabilities or integrations.

items.title: Allowed actions
items.description: Defines the specific operations and system interactions an agent is permitted to perform within bounded limits.
bullets: Define explicit action permissions and disallowed operations.
Restrict access to sensitive data sources and external systems.
Ensure agents follow predefined SOPs and escalation paths.
Limit write or modification capabilities unless explicitly approved.

items.title: Identity and blast radius
items.description: Ensures agents operate with identifiable identities and constrained permissions so their potential impact remains limited.
bullets: Assign unique identities to agents for traceability.
Limit agent privileges using scoped credentials.
Segment environments to prevent cross-system propagation.
Design agents so failures remain contained within bounded domains.

customSections.title: Human accountability
customSections.description: Despite automation, humans remain responsible for the actions of agentic systems. Governance must clearly define ownership, decision checkpoints, and escalation processes to maintain accountability.

items.title: Owners
items.description: Defines accountable individuals responsible for the deployment, monitoring, and outcomes of an agentic system.
bullets: Assign a responsible owner for every deployed agent.
Ensure owners understand the agent's capabilities and limitations.
Maintain clear responsibility for monitoring and operational oversight.
Require owners to approve changes to agent scope or permissions.

items.title: Approval points
items.description: Introduces human checkpoints where agent actions must be reviewed or approved before execution.
bullets: Require approval before executing high-impact actions.
Define escalation triggers for uncertain or ambiguous tasks.
Route sensitive operations to human decision makers.
Implement staged approvals for complex workflows.

items.title: Escalation
items.description: Provides structured processes for agents to defer uncertain decisions or failures to human operators.
bullets: Escalate actions when confidence thresholds are not met.
Trigger escalation for policy conflicts or unexpected results.
Notify operators when agents exceed predefined limits.
Provide clear channels for human intervention.

items.title: Override authority
items.description: Ensures humans retain the ability to intervene, halt, or override agent behavior when necessary.
bullets: Allow administrators to immediately stop agent execution.
Provide manual override for critical decision points.
Enable operators to revoke permissions or disable agents.
Maintain procedures for emergency shutdown.

customSections.title: Technical controls
customSections.description: Technical safeguards enforce governance policies through architecture, system design, and automated enforcement mechanisms to ensure agents operate safely.

items.title: Policy as code
items.description: Encodes governance policies into enforceable technical rules that automatically constrain agent behavior.
bullets: Define operational policies in machine-enforceable formats.
Validate agent actions against policy constraints.
Integrate policy checks into execution workflows.
Continuously test policies against new scenarios.

items.title: Least privilege
items.description: Restricts agent access to only the resources and permissions necessary to perform assigned tasks.
bullets: Grant minimal permissions required for task completion.
Use scoped credentials for external systems.
Separate read and write capabilities where possible.
Regularly review and revoke unnecessary privileges.

items.title: Evaluations
items.description: Implements testing and performance checks to validate agent behavior against accuracy, safety, and policy requirements.
bullets: Evaluate task execution accuracy and policy adherence.
Test agents against representative operational scenarios.
Measure tool-use accuracy and system interaction reliability.
Continuously benchmark performance across deployments.

items.title: Rollback and containment
items.description: Provides mechanisms to quickly stop, revert, or isolate agent actions when failures or risks are detected.
bullets: Implement mechanisms to halt agent workflows.
Enable rollback of automated changes or actions.
Contain failures within controlled system boundaries.
Establish incident response procedures for agent failures.

customSections.title: Transparency & compliance
customSections.description: Effective governance requires visibility into agent behavior and decision making. Logging, traceability, and reporting mechanisms ensure organizations can audit and validate outcomes.

items.title: Audit trails
items.description: Records the actions taken by agents, including inputs, outputs, and decisions, to support oversight and regulatory compliance.
bullets: Log all agent actions and system interactions.
Record decision inputs, outputs, and reasoning context.
Maintain immutable records for compliance review.
Track policy adherence and execution results.

items.title: Decision reconstruction
items.description: Enables organizations to understand how and why an agent made a particular decision or action.
bullets: Capture prompts, tools used, and decision pathways.
Retain execution context for analysis.
Support post-incident analysis and investigation.
Ensure traceability from decision to outcome.

items.title: Dashboards
items.description: Provides real-time visibility into agent operations, performance, and policy compliance.
bullets: Monitor agent activity and task execution metrics.
Track policy violations and escalation events.
Visualize performance and operational health.
Provide alerts for abnormal agent behavior.

items.title: Verification
items.description: Ensures that agent outputs and actions meet defined accuracy, safety, and compliance requirements.
bullets: Validate outputs against expected task criteria.
Check compliance with defined policies and procedures.
Implement verification steps for sensitive outputs.
Use automated tests to confirm behavior reliability.

customSections.title: Enablement & adaptation
customSections.description: Governance must evolve as organizations adopt agentic systems. Training, operational learning, and continuous re-evaluation help teams adapt responsibly.

items.title: Training
items.description: Equips employees and operators with the knowledge needed to design, supervise, and manage agentic systems responsibly.
bullets: Educate teams on agent capabilities and limitations.
Train operators on governance procedures and oversight.
Provide guidance on safe system integration.
Ensure staff understand escalation and incident protocols.

items.title: Responsible use
items.description: Promotes organizational practices that encourage safe and ethical deployment of agentic technologies.
bullets: Establish guidelines for acceptable agent usage.
Promote awareness of risks and ethical considerations.
Encourage responsible experimentation within guardrails.
Document best practices for agent development.

items.title: Incident learning
items.description: Uses operational failures and unexpected behavior to improve governance, policies, and technical safeguards.
bullets: Investigate incidents involving agent decisions or actions.
Update policies based on operational lessons learned.
Share findings across teams to prevent recurrence.
Continuously refine risk management practices.

items.title: Re-qualification
items.description: Ensures agents remain compliant and effective over time through periodic reassessment and validation.
bullets: Reassess agent risk classification periodically.
Retest agents when models or integrations change.
Verify continued policy compliance and safety.
Require re-approval for expanded capabilities.

title: Platform & Tooling
summary: Provides the engineering substrate that makes agentic delivery repeatable, observable, secure, and scalable across teams.
whyItMatters: Agentic engineering only scales when the underlying platform supplies clean environments, reusable pipelines, trusted context, tool access boundaries, and operational telemetry. Otherwise every team improvises and the model becomes fragile.
executiveQuestions: What platform capabilities are required before agents can be trusted in delivery?
How will teams get consistent environments, pipelines, and controls?
Where will agents retrieve trusted context and system knowledge?
How will platform services enforce standards without slowing teams down?
What parts of the platform should be productized for reuse?
keyDecisions: Choose the engineering workbench and runtime surfaces for humans and agents
Standardize CI/CD, IaC, security scanning, testing, and observability services
Define context sources, knowledge boundaries, and retrieval patterns
Create reference architectures, templates, and reusable automation
Determine what becomes part of the internal developer platform
majorActivities: Create reusable repos, templates, prompts, and environment baselines
Build secure tool integrations for source control, testing, cloud, and ticketing
Establish artifact, evidence, and runbook storage patterns
Implement telemetry for agents, workflows, approvals, and releases
Productize the most repeatable capabilities into the platform

title: People & Enablement
summary: Builds the organizational capability to adopt, supervise, and scale agentic engineering rather than treating it as a one-time implementation.
whyItMatters: Most organizations want to learn this model while delivering with it. People and enablement make the difference between a successful pilot and an enduring operating capability that clients can run themselves.
executiveQuestions: What roles need to change as agentic delivery matures?
How will teams learn to supervise, review, and collaborate with agents?
What communities, coaching, and change practices are required?
How do we preserve deep engineering and domain judgment while increasing automation?
How will adoption and proficiency be measured?
keyDecisions: Define the future-state role model for business, engineering, platform, and operations
Choose the training, coaching, and paired-delivery approach
Set learning paths for developers, architects, reviewers, and operators
Establish communities of practice and stewardship roles
Determine how adoption and proficiency will be measured and reinforced
majorActivities: Create role-based enablement tracks
Deliver paired implementation with client teams
Publish playbooks, office hours, and reusable examples
Run communities of practice around prompts, patterns, and controls
Track usage, confidence, proficiency, and workflow adoption

title: Human-Agent Collaboration
summary: Defines how humans and specialized agents coordinate work, exchange context, and maintain accountability across the engineering lifecycle.
whyItMatters: The core operating challenge is not just having agents. It is designing how work is decomposed, supervised, handed off, reviewed, escalated, and learned from. Collaboration design turns isolated AI usage into a governed production system.
executiveQuestions: What work should remain primarily human-led versus agent-led?
How are tasks decomposed and coordinated across agents?
What artifacts structure handoffs between humans and agents?
When must humans intervene, approve, or redirect work?
How will we prevent context loss, duplication, and uncontrolled autonomy?
keyDecisions: Define the coordinator or supervisor pattern
Assign specialist agent roles across planning, implementation, testing, release, and operations
Standardize work products such as briefs, specs, review packets, and evidence packs
Design escalation paths and exception handling between humans and agents
Determine where collaboration data and memory should be stored
majorActivities: Define agent responsibilities and non-goals
Create structured briefs and acceptance criteria for handoffs
Build work decomposition patterns for parallel and sequential tasks
Instrument collaboration flows for traceability and learning
Tune the interaction model based on review bottlenecks and failure modes

title: Measurement & Value
summary: Connects agentic engineering to business outcomes, delivery performance, risk reduction, and capability adoption so the model can be governed and scaled.
whyItMatters: If value is not measured, agentic engineering will be judged by anecdotes. Measurement creates the fact base for investment, governance, prioritization, and gradual expansion of autonomy.
executiveQuestions: What business outcomes justify the investment?
How will we measure speed, quality, reliability, and risk?
What evidence shows the organization is becoming more capable?
How will we distinguish real improvement from novelty effects?
What signals determine where to scale next?
keyDecisions: Select the core delivery, quality, reliability, and adoption metrics
Define baselines and comparison periods
Establish how to measure agent effectiveness and human oversight quality
Map metrics to executive, delivery, and operational dashboards
Use evidence to govern expansion, remediation, and prioritization
majorActivities: Define baseline metrics before major rollout
Create executive and operational dashboards
Measure human and agent performance together rather than separately
Feed results back into governance, platform, and enablement improvements
Use value data to shape commercialization and scale-up decisions
